Auto-fixing ESLint Errors in VSCode (on Save and on Format)
This post is a quick walkthrough on getting VSCode to auto-fix ESLint warnings and errors, including the formatting side of things.
ESLint later decided to drop its code-formatting features, so going forward ESLint shouldn't conflict with Prettier as much. I've also written a Prettier intro:
You'll end up using Prettier anyway — why not start with it? (article in Mandarin)
. I'm leaving this post as-is though, since some older projects still apply.
A note before diving in: if you're not confident with JavaScript yet, or you're a brand new dev, I'd strongly recommend
using this feature. Over-relying on tools before you understand how to fix errors yourself weakens your fundamentals and leaves you stuck when VSCode can't auto-fix something.
This feature is more suited for:
People with solid fundamentals who want to learn JS via ESLint
People who want to enforce a consistent code style on themselves
If you're either of those, this is great.
Two ways to set this up.
The simplest: enable ESLint auto-fix globally. Every save triggers a fix.
If you can't find that, use the shortcut:
language-json shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
Now open a project full of ESLint errors and hit save — you'll see them auto-fix.
Sometimes you only want this for a specific project, not your whole machine. In that case, create a
This folder holds project-scoped VSCode settings — same concept as the global settings, just scoped to one project.
Same auto-fix-on-save behaviour as the global setting.
The other approach is to use VSCode's Format Document feature.
The catch is VSCode's built-in formatter uses Prettier style, which conflicts with ESLint. Most people install the
extension (some VSCode versions install it by default — though mine didn't, so I haven't run into that). But Prettier's style and ESLint's style clash.
After installing Prettier ESLint, the first time you hit the format shortcut, this dialog pops up.
Click Configure and pick Prettier ESLint.
That resolves the Prettier vs ESLint conflict.
If the dialog never shows up, open
Some people have it set to VSCode's TS-language formatter by default, which is why the dialog doesn't show:
You might be wondering what the actual difference is between these two approaches:

Short version: the save-time fix kicks in when you save, so it depends on ESLint warnings being present — if no ESLint warning, no fix. The format-document approach reformats according to ESLint rules every time you trigger the format command.
Which is better? Whichever fits your workflow.
